Day 13 - Put a Helmet on Your Express!
Put a Helmet on Your Express
We covered CORS and Env in the previous posts — those are basic basics. Now let's introduce something special called Helmet.
Before getting into Helmet, we need to clarify something — about "web security".
Web information security is a huge topic. This post can't cover everything — web security includes things like personal account password registration rules. So this post focuses on backend security.
The Helmet docs explicitly mention: Helmet is middleware that helps protect your Express application. It sets various HTTP headers that help your application avoid known web attacks.
In short, Helmet puts a Helmet on your Express, so your app can avoid known web attacks — just like a person wearing a helmet can avoid known injuries.
, it auto-adds things to your HTTP headers that help your app avoid known web attacks.
to HTTP headers. This tells attackers your app is built with Express, so they can target Express-specific attacks.

In real-world dev, we avoid letting attackers know what we used to build the app — when that app has a vulnerability, attackers can target the vulnerability. So basic hiding is necessary. Early in my career working with WordPress, I encountered attackers targeting WordPress vulnerabilities — so this is really important.
Helmet hides this header for you, so attackers can't tell your app uses Express.
Don't believe it? Try the Express we practiced earlier with Postman, and you'll see:

Clickjacking is an attack where you're tricked into clicking an element on a webpage (usually a button or link). It's done by placing a transparent overlay on the page, so you think you're clicking one element but you're actually clicking another.
to HTTP headers, which prevents your page from being embedded in other pages and clickjacked.
If that's hard to picture, here's example code:
language-html shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Click here to win a prize!
"opacity: 0; position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%;"
So when you click "Click here to win a prize!", you're actually clicking the hacker's site, not the page you wanted.
to HTTP headers, preventing this when your page is embedded in another page.
, meaning only same-origin pages can embed it. See more

You may have seen this term — if not familiar with the Chinese name, CSRF might be more familiar. It's an attack where the attacker injects malicious things into your page. When you click, the attacker's malicious code triggers.
I won't go into much detail since I've written about it before:
Full-Stack Warrior's Path Node.js Basics - CSRF (article in Mandarin)

Early defense was to add CSRF Tokens to forms. But the modern mainstream is frontend-backend separation, so we use Client Double Submit Cookie. This adds a CSRF Token to the Cookie, then puts the same CSRF Token in HTTP headers. When the backend receives the request, it compares the Cookie's CSRF Token to the HTTP header's CSRF Token — same means legit, different means illegit. Implementation reference

This attack is pretty common in dev — also called Cross-Site Scripting, abbreviated XSS. Most commonly seen in Input fields, where attackers input malicious scripts. When you click submit, the malicious script runs somewhere — could be your page, could be another page.
attribute comes from user input, and the user input is a malicious script. When the page loads, the malicious script runs. Concept: user inputs:

How to use Helmet in Express?
Using Helmet is super simple. Just install the Helmet package:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Then use Helmet in your code:

Express's official Issues had a debate about whether to remove the

Even though Helmet does its best to put a helmet on your Express, don't think Helmet is a silver bullet. Security incidents are constant, and attackers keep finding vulnerabilities. So we still have to pay attention — don't ignore security just because Helmet exists.
